segments.s255-g33: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a circumstantial clause. دانی is 2nd-person singular present 'you know'. که introduces the content clause. In the embedded clause بر تو نماند جهان the verb نماند (3rd sg.) carries the negative sense 'does not remain / will not remain', تو is the indirect object 'for you', and جهان (the subject) follows the verb — a common classical/poetic word order.
segments.s255-g34: چه here is rhetorical ('how/why' or 'what are you doing'), followed by پیچی, 2nd-singular present 'you turn' / 'you twist'. تو is the explicit 2nd-person subject (emphatic). زان = 'from that', جای = 'place'. نوشین qualifies جای ('sweet, pleasant'), and روان is the noun 'soul / spirit' addressed vocatively (O soul) or as the thing concerned; overall the clause asks why you turn away from that sweet place (addressing the soul).
segments.s255-g35: بخور is an imperative (2nd-singular) 'eat' / 'consume'. آنچه is a relative/pronominal 'that which / whatever' and داری is 2nd-person present 'you have' (آنچه داری = 'what you have'). و connects the clauses. بیشی is 'more / excess' (surplus) and مجوی is a prohibitive form (2nd-singular negative) 'do not seek' — so the clause means 'Eat what you have and do not seek excess.'
segments.s255-g36: که is causal 'for / because'. از introduces the cause 'from / because of'. آز is the noun 'greed'. کاهد is the (classical) 3rd-person singular of کاهیدن 'to lessen / diminish' (here present: 'diminishes'); همی is an older aspectual particle meaning roughly 'always / continually' or emphasizing the ongoing action. آبروی is the object 'honor / reputation'. Together: 'for greed always diminishes honor.'
segments.s255-g1: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'/'as'); جهان is the subject 'the world'. به زاری is a prepositional phrase where به = 'toward/into' and زاری = 'lamentation'. برآید is the verb 'arise/appear' in the present/epic narrative form, and همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle common in Ferdowsi that cushions the verb (here following it) to give habitual or ongoing sense.
segments.s255-g2: بد و نیک form a coordinated pair 'bad and good' that functions as the theme of روزی. روزی here is used in the sense of an occurrence/fortune that 'dawns' or 'appears'. سرآید is the verbal predicate 'will come/occur' (third person), and the final همی again gives the archaic continuous/emphatic flavor — 'will come to pass'. Word order places the predicate at the end, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s255-g3: چو introduces a temporal/conditional protasis ('when' or 'if'). بستی is the perfect/past form 'girded' used here as a general/habitual action ('when one girds'). کمر 'waist/loins' is the direct object of بستی in the idiom 'بستن کمر' = 'to gird one's loins'. بر indicates direction/purpose ('for, toward') and combines with در راه 'in/on the path' (در = 'in'), while آز is the goal noun 'greed'.
segments.s255-g4: شود is the impersonal/resultative present 'become/happen'. کار 'affair/work' is the head noun, and گیتیت is 'گیتی' (world) with the enclitic -ت (your) forming a genitive/possessive 'your world' or 'the world of you' — i.e., 'your worldly affairs'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/continually' modifying the following adjective دراز 'long', so یکسر دراز means 'continually long' or 'prolonged altogether'. The verb و predicate order follows classical Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s255-g5: This clause uses an infinitival/gerundial construction: به یک روی ('at/with one face/one stroke') modifies جستن ('to leap/attempt'); بلندی is the goal 'height/loftiness' (here the object/goal of the leap). سزاست is the impersonal present 'it is fitting/appropriate' (literally 'it befits' or 'it is deserved'), so the whole clause says that attempting a single swift leap to height is proper.
segments.s255-g6: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. در میان is a locative phrase 'in the middle/in the midst'. دم here means 'tip/edge' (used metaphorically as 'tail tip') rather than 'breath', and اژدهاست is the noun 'dragon' with the copular/adjective ending ('is a dragon' / 'dragon is there') — together, 'if there is a dragon's tail in the middle'. This conditional qualifies the preceding claim.
segments.s255-g7: و دیگر که begins a connective clause 'and furthermore / moreover, because'. گیتی ندارد درنگ is literal 'the world has no delay' i.e. 'the world does not wait / there is no staying in the world' — ندارد is the present negative 'does not have', and درنگ is the noun 'delay/pausing'. The clause sets up the reason for the following statement.
segments.s255-g8: سرای here means 'house/dwelling' (note: the provided lemma senses are verbs; context shows a noun meaning 'dwelling'), سپنجی is an adjective 'spongy/fragile' describing the dwelling. چه ... و چه ... is the correlative 'whether ... or ...' (used here with چه پهن و چه تنگ = 'whether wide or narrow'). The phrase as a whole says that, given the world's lack of pause, a frail dwelling — regardless of being spacious or cramped — is of no consequence.
segments.s255-g17: سر مایه is a compound: سر (here 'chief, principal') + مایه ('resource, capital'), so 'the principal resource (of)'. مرد is the possessor 'man'. سنگ و خرد are two coordinated nouns ('stone/firmness' and 'wisdom'); و simply links them. Word order is noun‑phrase (possessor phrase) followed by coordinated items describing the 'capital'.
segments.s255-g18: ز introduces origin ('from, derived from') + گیتی ('the world'). بی‌آزاری is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'without harming / harmlessness' modifying گیتی. اندر + خورد here form a light verbal expression: اندر 'in/into' + خورد (from خوردن) used poetically in the sense 'befall/come' — together meaning 'is taken/comes from'.
segments.s255-g19: در دانش is a prepositional phrase 'in knowledge/learning'. آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon' and is linked by و to form a two‑part standard list: 'in learning and then in uprightness' (راستی is a noun here meaning moral truth/uprightness). The phrase sets up two qualities to be sought.
segments.s255-g20: گرین is a contracted/poetic form here (effectively 'گر این' = 'if these') rather than the literal 'tearful/green' (so no supplied sense fits exactly). نیابی is the negative subjunctive/optative form of 'yāftan' ('you do not find/attain'); it carries both lexical meaning 'find/attain' and grammatical subjunctive coloring. روان is 'soul/spirit' and کاستی is a nominal predicate meaning 'deficiency / you will lack'. The clause thus gives the conditional result: 'if you do not find these two, your soul is deficient.'
segments.s255-g21: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. خود is emphatic 'yourself' or 'you personally'. بمانی is present/subjunctive of ماندن 'you remain/stay'. به گیتی دراز is a temporal/locative phrase 'long in the world' (به + گیتی + adjective دراز), giving duration/location: 'remain long in the world.'
segments.s255-g22: ز introduces cause/source 'from / because of'. رنج تن is a genitival phrase 'the suffering of the body' (رنج = suffering; تن = body). آید is the present/impersonal 'comes' ('arises'). به + رفتن نیاز is the object of آید: 'a need to go/ depart arises' — here رفتن in context carries the idiomatic sense 'depart (die)' (so 'a need to depart').
segments.s255-g23: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one / a certain (thing)'. ژرف is the adjective 'deep' modifying دریـاست, a fused form of دریا + است ('is a sea'). بن ناپدید is a noun phrase 'its bottom is unseen' (بن = bottom/base; ناپدید = invisible/vanished). The line uses inverted poetic word order: predicate and nominal phrases are juxtaposed for effect.
segments.s255-g24: در گنج is a locative/genitive phrase 'in/with the treasure'. رازش is راز + enclitic ش 'its secret'. ندارد is the negative of داشتن 'does not have/possess'. کلید is the noun 'key'. The phrase means 'there is no key to its secret' (i.e. the treasure's secret has no key).
segments.s255-g25: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. چند here means 'some/a little' (an indefinite quantity) modifying یابی. یابی is 2nd-person present/imperative-type form 'you obtain/you find'. فزون is a postpositive adjective 'more/greater'. بایدت is باید with the enclitic -ت 'to you', meaning 'it is necessary for you' or 'you must'. The whole segment reads: 'If you obtain some extra, you must...'.
segments.s255-g26: همان is the demonstrative 'that same'. خورده is a past-participle/adjectival use 'eaten/consumed' used substantively ('that which has been eaten'). یک روز is temporal 'one day' (here adverbial: 'one day, at some time'). بگزایدت is an old/subjunctive-style third-person form of گزیدن with enclitic -ت 'to you' and here expresses what will happen in time (literally 'a day will choose/bring/give/take to you'); in context it carries the sense 'one day will take it from you'.
segments.s255-g27: سه چیزت literally 'three things' with the possessive/enclitic -ت 'for you' (or 'you have to do with'). بباید is the impersonal/present form meaning 'are necessary' / 'you must have'. کز is the archaic relative 'که از' or 'from which/for which'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' (referring forward to what follows). چاره نیست is the phrase 'there is no remedy/solution' (چاره = remedy, نیست = does not exist).
segments.s255-g28: وزو here is an archaic contracted sequence (literary 'و زِ آن' = 'and from that'); it functions like 'and from them/it'. بر سرت is a prepositional phrase 'upon your head' (بر = on/upon, سرت = your head). نیز 'also/too' adds emphasis. پیغاره is a message/notice/warning, and نیست negates it ('there is no warning'). The phrase therefore says: 'and from them no warning falls upon your head.'
segments.s255-g29: خوری‌گر is an agent noun 'a glutton' (one who eats/drinks). بپوشی is the subjunctive/conditional form of پوشیدن 'to clothe/cover' (here 'if you clothe'). و‌گر is the fused 'و اگر' = 'and if'. گستری is the subjunctive/imperative-form of گستردن 'to spread' (here 'you spread' or 'you lay out'). The clause sets up two conditional actions: 'if you clothe a glutton, or if you spread [for him]...'.
segments.s255-g30: سزد‌گر is an adjectival/agentive form related to سزدن 'to be fitting' — here 'if it is fitting' or 'one who judges fit'. به دیگر سخن is a prepositional phrase 'to/for other speech' meaning 'to another's words/opinion'. ننگری is the negative-subjunctive/optative form (archaic) where ن- prefixes the subjunctive نگری, giving 'do not look/heed/consider'. Together: 'If it seems fitting, do not heed another's words.'
segments.s255-g31: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when' (or 'if'). زین here is a literary contraction corresponding to 'از این' ('of/from these' or simply 'these'); this is an archaic use (listed dictionary senses for زین as 'saddle' do not fit the grammatical role, so treat as - از این). گذشتی is the 2nd-person past 'you passed/you went beyond'. همه 'all' modifies the nouns رنج و آز 'suffering and greed/avarice'. The clause: 'When you have passed these three, all is suffering and greed.'
segments.s255-g32: The correlative چه ... چه ... is 'whether ... or ...'. در آز پیچی literally 'you turn/act in greed' (در = in, آز = greed, پیچی = you turn/act — here a verbal noun/form). چه اندر نیاز is the parallel 'or in need' (اندر = in/within, نیاز = need). The line contrasts acting from avarice versus acting from necessity.
segments.s255-g9: پرستنده‌ی آز و جویای کین is a coordinated noun phrase linked by ezāfe and conjunction: پرستنده‌ی + noun (آز) = 'devotee/worshipper of greed' (پرستنده with the genitive ezāfe ی). سپس 'و جویای کین' is appended with جویای functioning as a present-participle adjective 'seeking' modifying an elided person, and کین is its object 'vengeance.' Word order is noun + ezāfe + object, then conjunction + adjective + object, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s255-g10: به گیتی ز کس نشنود آفرین is an adverbial/negative-clause: به گیتی ('in/for the world') sets the domain, ز کس = 'from anyone,' نشنود is the negative third-person present (ن‑ prefix + شنود) 'does not hear/is not told,' and آفرین is the object 'praise/acclamation.' The verb appears before its object (poetic order), and the negative prefix n- is the classical form.
segments.s255-g11: چو + clause is a temporal 'when/if' conjunction (چو = 'when'). سرو سهی گوژ combines noun + poetic epithet (سرو 'cypress', سهی 'dark/black' as an adjectival epithet) and گوژ 'hunchbacked' as a following adjective; گردد (گردد/گشت) is the classical present 'becomes.' The postposed locative به باغ 'in/to the garden' marks where this change happens.
segments.s255-g12: بدو is a fused prepositional phrase (به + او) meaning 'to it/upon it.' بر شود is a light-verb construction meaning 'is raised/comes upon' (بر = on/upon; شود = becomes/is made). تیره روشن چراغ is a compressed epithet: two adjectives (تیره 'dark' and روشن 'bright/clear') stacked before the noun چراغ 'lamp'; poetic juxtaposition gives the paradoxical 'dark-and-bright lamp.'
segments.s255-g13: کند here is the verb 'makes/does' (causative/transitive use), with برگ پژمرده 'leaves (برگ) withered (پژمرده)' as the direct object and بیخ سست 'root (بیخ) weak (سست)' coordinated by و. The verb is foregrounded and governs two coordinated noun phrases describing the effect.
segments.s255-g14: سرش = سر + enclitic pronoun ش 'its' = 'its head.' سوی 'toward' marks the direction; پستی is a noun/adjective meaning 'lowness/low status' or 'meanness' here 'the low ground/lowliness.' گراید (گراید/گراییدن) is a classical present 'turns/ inclines.' نخست 'first' indicates that this bending is the initial effect. The construction is subject (implied tree/it) → possessive phrase → directional verb phrase.
segments.s255-g15: بروید ز خاک uses بروید (here 'appears/rises, shows its face') + ز خاک 'from the earth' describing emergence; و شود باز خاک follows with شود 'becomes' and باز 'again' plus خاک 'earth' — 'becomes earth again.' This is a compressed poetic cycle: appear from earth → return to earth, with شدن as the copular/becoming verb.
segments.s255-g16: همه جای is an adverbial 'everywhere' (همه + جای 'place'); the predicate is a noun-list ترسست و تیمار و باک where the copula (is/there is) is omitted as is common in poetry, so it reads 'there is fear, solicitude, and dread everywhere.' ترسست here functions as the first nominal element of the predicate.

sentences.s255-s17: When you know that the world will not remain with you, why do you turn away from that sweet place, O soul?
sentences.s255-s18: Eat what you have and seek no surplus, for greed always lessens one's honor.
sentences.s255-s1: When the world breaks out in lamentation, both ill and good fortunes will arise.
sentences.s255-s2: When one girds the loins for the road of greed, the affairs of your world will become continuously long.
sentences.s255-s3: It is fitting to leap up at a single stroke, if a dragon's tail lies in the midst.
sentences.s255-s4: And further, since the world allows no delay, a fragile (spongy) dwelling — whether broad or narrow — matters not.
sentences.s255-s9: A man's chief wealth — firmness (stone) and wisdom — is derived from a world free of harm.
sentences.s255-s10: In learning, and thereafter in uprightness: if you do not attain these two, your soul will be lacking.
sentences.s255-s11: If you yourself remain long in the world, bodily suffering will bring a need to depart.
sentences.s255-s12: One is a deep sea with an unseen bottom; the treasure's secret has no key.
sentences.s255-s13: Even if you obtain more, you must remember that that which has been consumed will one day be taken from you.
sentences.s255-s14: There are three things you must have for which there is no remedy, and from them no warning comes upon your head.
sentences.s255-s15: If you clothe a glutton, or if you spread (a carpet) for him, then — if it seems fitting — do not heed another's words.
sentences.s255-s16: When you have passed these three, all is toil and avarice — whether you act from greed or from need.
sentences.s255-s5: One who is a worshipper of greed and a seeker of vengeance will not hear praise from anyone in the world.
sentences.s255-s6: When the dark cypress becomes hunchbacked in the garden, upon it a lamp — both dark and bright — is kindled.
sentences.s255-s7: It makes the leaves withered and the root weak; its head first bends toward lowliness.
sentences.s255-s8: It rises from the earth and again becomes earth; everywhere there is fear, solicitude, and dread.
